reasoning:
Response A is stronger on nearly every axis because it is much more concrete, operationally grounded, and explicit about trade-offs. It provides a full end-to-end design with component responsibilities, deployment topology, crypto primitives, message flows, invite lifecycle, schema-level data model, realtime protocol details, backup/restore, and a detailed threat model.

Response B has a solid high-level structure and some good choices (Go + SQLite + WebSocket), but it is more superficial and occasionally inconsistent. Its crypto section mixes “Signal + MLS extensions” without enough implementation precision for a small self-hosted system, and some details are questionable (e.g., server-signed identity keys framing, vague MLS handling). Its invite flow is interesting but overcomplicated for the constraints and less clearly tied to auditable chain behavior and operational realities than A.

A is not perfect: it explicitly weakens initial-session FS by skipping one-time prekeys and uses a simpler non-FS group key model. However, those compromises are acknowledged, and the design remains much more actionable and complete than B. On balance, A better satisfies the prompt’s requirement for a thorough, precise system design document tailored to a single-VPS self-hosted community app.
